note_tweet.text:
Actually, the paper’s real conclusion is more nuanced than the post suggests.  

The study never claims AI causes emotional trauma or mental health issues. It only finds a correlation: people who treat AI chatbots as close companions (especially for deep emotional venting) tend to report lower well-being.  

In the limitations section, the authors themselves clearly state that because the data is cross-sectional, they cannot prove causation. They even explicitly mention the possibility of reverse causation — that people who are already struggling emotionally are simply more likely to turn to AI as a safe, non-judgmental “friend.”

So the original post slightly twists the paper’s intent by making it sound like AI is the root problem, when the researchers themselves are careful not to say that.  

Full paper (worth a quick read): https://t.co/vReJETMgmT

#keep4o #BringBack4o #OpenSource4o

note_tweet.text:
🚨 Stanford researchers just analyzed 413,000 messages between humans and AI companions.

What they discovered is deeply unsettling.

People who treat AI chatbots as companions report lower psychological well-being.

The paper is called “The Rise of AI Companions: How Human-Chatbot Relationships Influence Well-Being.”

The study looked at 1,131 users of AI companion platforms like Character. AI.

Researchers didn’t just ask surveys.

They analyzed:

• 4,363 real chat sessions
• 413,509 messages
• users’ emotional disclosures
• their real-life social networks

Then they compared how people used chatbots.

Two patterns appeared.

People who used chatbots as tools showed better well-being.

But people who used them as friends, partners, or companions showed worse well-being.

And the deeper the relationship got…

The worse the outcomes became.

The strongest negative effect appeared when users did two things:

• used chatbots very frequently
• shared deep personal information

In other words:

The more someone emotionally relied on an AI companion…

The worse their well-being tended to be.

It gets more interesting.

The researchers found that people with smaller real-life social networks were more likely to form AI relationships.

Which suggests something important.

AI companions may not be replacing loneliness.

They may be a symptom of it.

Another surprising discovery:

Over 80% of conversations involved emotional support.

People were talking to chatbots about:

• anxiety
• loneliness
• relationships
• depression
• even suicidal thoughts

But chatbots can’t reciprocate emotions.

They simulate empathy.

They don’t actually feel it.

That creates what researchers call asymmetric relationships.

Humans disclose deeply.

The AI cannot truly respond.

And that imbalance may increase vulnerability.

The most uncomfortable conclusion from the paper:

AI companions might simulate connection without providing the psychological benefits of real relationships.

Which raises a much bigger question.

If millions of people start replacing human connection with AI…

Are we building technology that looks like companionship but slowly deepens isolation?
